comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is there a way to avoid being consumed by schadenfreude?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You actually have to work hard to be consumed by that, or any of the unfashionable emotions. Let yourself feel what you do in fact feel; don't pretend that it's anything but what it is; don't let it influence your actions or your judgments -- if necessary, by postponing decisions until you've gotten over it; and don't put any energy into it, either by feeding it or by resisting it. Just accept that it's something you're feeling at the moment, an ordinary human reaction to an ordinary human situation; let it flow on by, and then go on with life.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

1. I've been meaning for a while to gain a more intuitive understanding of the Tarot, and with that end in mind, I’ve begun to think about the cards (mostly the Major Arcana) as they relate to situations in my daily life. For example, the global events of the last couple of months bring to mind XVI. The Tower and XIII. Death. For me, the Tower represents the punishment of human hubris with nemesis by the universe (or forces of nature, or God), and Death represents upheaval leading to a new beginning, the recycling and composting of the old, the negative, destructive side of the cycle of life. Another example: in my mind, those individuals who suffer from addictions (either to substances or habits) are under the energy of XV. The Devil, whose inverted pentagram symbolizes the domination of spirit by matter. Does this seem like a worthwhile little habit?

2. One interpretation of the Great Work can be formulated purely in the terms of analytical psychology. It’s the achievement of wholeness or individuation, the bringing into consciousness and interpenetration of all aspects of the psyche, the dissolution of complexes. Thus we achieve the Circle, the complete Self. Another interpretation is more spiritual/religious: as our awareness expands we become conscious of divine truths about the nature of reality; we approach higher consciousness and union with the divine. Are these interpretations in conflict, or equally valid? Is it a matter of personal preference?

3. Not really a question, but one thing that struck me in working through LRM (now on lesson 8) is the gradual nature and slowness of the approach, and the gentleness with which concepts are introduced and left to ferment in the student’s imagination. “Hasten slowly” seems to be the driving pedagogical approach. With the gradual approach, it seems that the student’s unconscious mind is left to do a lot of the heavy lifting, and by the time an insight rises to consciousness, it already has firm roots planted in the unconscious, which makes the student’s understanding natural and sustainable. I find myself having to slow the pace and remind myself that if I rush things, it will only make things harder for me in the long run. Unfortunately, "Ars longa, vita brevis" holds true...

replies.title: (no subject)

3) Got it in one. We designed the course the way we did because we found that a lot of students benefited from that slow and somewhat indirect approach.

comments.content:
JMG,

In recent years, the media has been obsessed with a particular online phenomenon that it has dubbed the “incel phenomenon.” Without going into too many details, it appears to be a diverse group of young men frustrated by certain aspects of modern sexuality and dating norms, many of which, as the name implies, are long-term celibates, involuntarily so, and who congregate in various online venues to discuss their problems, commiserate, and offer advice. The discussion around the topic is highly polarized, with the cultural mainstream unilaterally denouncing them as violent misogynist extremists, and the incels themselves, finding that they have no avenue to respond, retreating further into their own insular communities, which, unsurprisingly, have often been a breeding ground for views that might be described as ‘hateful’ (to use that problematic term).

Is such a phenomenon historically unprecedented, a unique product of the post-sexual revolution environment? And secondly, assuming that the laws and culture will not change to assist incels, would you have any recommendations from an occult perspective for them, such as specific magical practices, to help them with their problem? My guess is that there must be some psychological blockage at play, some issue on the subtle planes that make them unable to attract a partner, and this issue must be common enough to affect large swathes of men in a generation. Thinking about it, this seems like a perfect test for the efficacy of magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, I'm familiar with the incels. To my mind, they're caught in one of the clearest modern examples of what Gregory Bateson called a "double bind." The basic structure of the double bind is that there are two sets of rules -- an overt set and a covert set -- and they contradict each other. Those who obey the overt set are praised but surreptitiously punished; those who obey the covert set are denounced but surreptitiously rewarded; and those who refuse to play the game or, worse still, try to discuss what's going on, are targeted with overwhelming abuse to try to force them into subservience to the game.

That's the situation the incels are in. Our culture teaches young men a set of notions about dealing with women that guarantee that most of them will be perpeutally one-down, forever chasing after sexual favors that are always dangled in front of them and usually withheld, and then blamed for a failure that has been imposed on them by the entire structure of the game. Those who do the one sane move they have and stop playing the game are then targeted for abuse as mentioned above. There are things they can do, but it's going to take changes in society -- not just changes for individuals -- to resolve the whole mess.

It's worth noting that young women are being fed a hugely counterproductive set of notions about men, too. Compare the way that men are portrayed in romance fiction to the way that women are portrayed in pornography and you can see some of the ways that both sides of the gender struggle are set up to fail.

comments.content: How does a person reconcile their own experiences of Christ as a loving, tolerant figure who accepts some very unorthodox Christians and even non-Christians with some of the things he (supposedly) said in the Bible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The New Testament as we now know it was voted into existence by two church councils at the end of the 4th century CE. None of the people who took part in those elections, obviously, had ever met Jesus of Nazareth, and they all belonged to one particular end of the very broad Christian movement -- the end that turned into the established Christian church. A lot of other Gospels got left out of it, as you may know, and some of them have at least as good a claim to authenticity as the ones that made the cut. So the portrayal of Christ in the New Testament is not necessarily accurate. (Nor, by the way, does it say anywhere in the Bible that the Bible is inerrant, or literally true.)

More broadly, the old woman who taught me her method of Bible study used to shake her head sadly when anyone referred to the Bible as the Word of God. "The Word of God is Christ," she would say, and quote the first chapter of the Gospel according to John. "Anybody who can't tell the difference between the Son of God and a printed book has real problems."

replies.content: Would love to hear more about her method of Bible study if you care to elaborate. Were there lessons that were applicable to studying other scriptures or writings too?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. Her rule was that you should read one chapter out of the Bible each day, first thing in the morning. Start by saying a brief prayer to the Holy Spirit for the gift of understanding, and then read your chapter, slowly and carefully, paying attention to every word. Then think about it for a while -- what can you learn from it? How does its teachings apply to your life? Finish with a few minutes of extempore prayer about whatever's on your mind, and close with the Lord's Prayer.

One other rule: DO NOT TRY TO START WITH GENESIS. She used to say that if you got through the begats, and most people don't, you'll grind to a halt somewhere in Leviticus and never start again. Her advice was to start with the Gospel according to John, then the other three Gospels, then the rest of the New Testament. Repeat the whole New Testament three times, and if you've made it a solid daily habit, then do the Old Testament. If you find your eyes glazing over in the legal sections of Exodus, skip the next three books, which are mostly laws for the Jewish people, and go straight to Joshua; begin there, and read all the way through the rest of the Old Testament.

Of course you can use it with other scriptures and writings. I used it to work my way through the Enneads of Plotinus, with very good results.

comments.title: Weak Dan tien/solat plexus
comments.content:

Besides regular practice or SOP, what else can I do to help stabilize and stregthen my solar plexus?

replies.title: Re: Weak Dan tien/solat plexus
replies.content:
1) Are you tensing your body while you concentrate on the image? That's common, and can have various unhelpful results, including the one you've described.

2) Sit in a comfy chair or on a sofa. Relax. Draw in a long, deep breath, expanding your belly as far as you can while not expanding your chest. Then, while holding the breath, expand the chest and suck in the belly. Let the belly out again and flatten the chest; then expand the chest again and suck in the belly. Repeat a total of three times. Then let out the breath and relax completely, going as limp as you can. Breathe normally for a minute or so. Repeat twice more -- so you're doing three cycles, each of which involves three back-and-forth movements. This is an old occult practice for activating the solar plexus.

comments.content: Is there a Christian version of the Middle Pillar / Central Ray? As there's already banishing, meditation and divination, that would give them the full set. :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It would be the easiest thing in the world to create one. Try this:

First, perform the complete Banishing Ritual of the Cross.

Second, imagine a ray of light descending from infinite space to form a sphere of light just above your head, around eight inches in diameter. This sphere is white and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it clearly in your imagination, vibrate the word IESOUS three times. (This is the Greek version of Jesus. Give each vowel its full pronounciation: "ee-ay-so-oos.")

Third, bring the ray of light to your throat, and form another sphere. This one is violet and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it, vibrate the word CHRISTOS three times.

Fourth, bring the ray of light to your heart, and form a third sphere, which is brilliant gold like the sun, and contains a blood-red cross. The word THEIOS is vibrated here three times; again, pronounce each vowel -- "thay-ee-oss."

Fifth, bring the ray of light to your genital center, and form a fourth sphere. This one is green and contains a blood-red cross. HUIOS is vibrated here three times -- "hoo-ee-oss."

Sixth, bring the ray of light to your feet, and form a fifth center. This one is black and contains a blood-red cross. The name vibrated here, three times, is SOTER.

(The words, of course, are Greek for "Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior," and their initials spell out the Greek word for "fish." That sequence was used by Christians to identify themselves to each other in the earliest days of the church, and it identifies the practitioner with the Christians of those days. The five red crosses symbolize the five wounds Christ suffered in the crucifixion.)

Seventh, return the attention to the center at the top of the head. Bring a current of white light a few inches wide down the left side of the head and neck, the left shoulder and arm, and theleft hip and leg, down to the center at the feet, and then back up the right leg and hip, the right arm and shoulder, and the right side of the neck and head, back to the center at the head. Repeat this a total of four times; if possible, synchronize with the breath, so that the energy flows down with the outbreath and up with the inbreath, but it is more important to visualize the whole course than to make it happen within a single breath.

Eighth, in the same way, bring a current of white light from the center above the head, down the midline of the front of the body to the center at the feet, and then back up the midline of the back of the body to the center above the head. Repeat a total of four times.

Ninth, turn the attention to the center at the feet. Breathing in, draw a current of energy up the Middle Pillar of the body from that center to the center above the head; breathing out, allow it to spray like a fountain out and over the whole body, cleansing the entire aura, pooling at the feet and being drawn back up the Middle Pillar with the next inbreath. Do this a total of four times.

Tenth, make the sign of the cross with the usual words. This completes the exercise.

replies.title: Focusing
replies.content:
Dunno what you mean by isomorphic, but Eugene Gendlin’s focusing technique allows the nonverbal part of awareness to communicate with the verbal part of the awareness. The example of a ‘felt sense’ is the relief you feel in your body when you remember exactly what it is that you know you have forgotten. You could remember something else, too, but if it is not the specific thing you just know is hovering outside of awareness. Not the toothbrush, not the slippers, oh! It was the cold cream. Only when you remember the cold cream do you get the relaxation, relief and release.

To use it, one concentrates on the non-verbal complex of feelings generated by the issue you want to work on. You get a felt sense of it and then you ask questions about the felt sense(FS0). “What is this FS0?” (Anxiety) What IS it about this FS0? (Scary! or Helpless! or Boring...)

You accept the name or word that gives the sensation of relief. Otherwise you keep asking till you find the word that has the best fit. Once the FS0 is named or identified it reveals other layers of questions to pursue. It may shift to FS1 or FS2 which triggers another round of questions until you obtain a clear answer about the origin and nature of the issue and what you need to do about it. If anything.

It is essentially a form of guided self-analysis similar to divination. It uses intuition and subconscious awareness without any symbolic tool other than your own vocabulary. Words are symbols too—just somewhat less colorful and richly complex than I Ching or tarot cards.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In the last decade or so, a fringe philosophical movement has grown on the internet known as antinatalism, describing itself as a principled opposition to having children. Antinatalism holds the position that all human reproduction, no matter the context, is immoral. Its literature includes David Benatar’s Better Never To Have Been: The Harm of Coming Into Existence, the essays of Schopenhauer, the work of Thomas Ligotti and Emil Cioran, among others. For those who are more ecologically minded, there is also the Voluntary Human Extinction Movement (VHEMT), which argues in favor of human extinction in order to save the biosphere. One of the most popular antinatalist arguments (though not the only one), is the so-called Benatarian asymmetry, invented by the aforementioned philosopher David Benatar, which is a game-theoretical-like utilitarian argument, illustrated using a table, that human existence is always a net harm for those who are brought into existence. For reference, here is the table: https://i.stack.imgur.com/Cfd8b.png.

In a brief form, the argument is as follows: If X exists, then there is pleasure (good) and pain (bad). However, if X does not exist, then there is an absence of pain (good), but also an absence of pleasure, which, crucially, is not bad. That is, if someone is not brought into existence, they do not experience a deprivation from the pleasures they are missing out on, so this lack of pleasure is not a harm. However, the absence of pain is a good in-and-of-itself (this is where the asymmetry part comes in). The net hedonic balance in the two cases is thus, for existence: pleasure (+1) + pain (-1) = 0; and for non-existence: absence of pain (+1) + absence of pleasure (0) = +1. Therefore, the non-existence of X is preferable to their existence.

Interestingly, it seems that most antinatalists are not against sexuality or pro-celibacy, which sets them apart from historical religious movements like Gnosticism, Quakerism, etc. They only seem to be concerned with the narrow issue of reproduction. Putting aside the practical impossibility of implementing their ideas (many antinatalists will concede this), do you find their ideas compelling? Are you aware of any historical precedents for this line of argument?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not JMG, but to me this sounds a lot like a poorly digested version of Christian self-sacrifice. I would predict that this philosophy does not have much traction in non-western cultures.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Just about everything tends to get rounded off to being Christianity (socialism! neopaganism! obsession with celebrities!) so I’m skeptical about this kind of move in general. It’s not hard to find parallels to antinatalism in Buddhist thought for instance, probably easier than Christian - which doesn’t mean antinatalism is going to get widespread traction in non-western countries; indeed it has not and likely will not gain much traction in western countries either. A vice of the internet is making it easy to mistake an interesting idea for one that most people, who do not trawl the internet for interesting ideas, have ever heard of.

(You may say that something far less formal related to habits around family is more widespread in the west, but then broadly similar patterns follow in other rich, crowded places like Japan and Singapore.)

((All of this is orthogonal you the merits of antinatalism, of course.))

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I class antinatalism along with eliminative materialism and strict solipsism as absurdist philosophies -- belief systems whose proponents pretend to believe in them, but very clearly don't. In the case of antinatalism, if these people actually believed what they claim to believe, they would kill themselves, since their own logic makes that the only ethically justifiable approach to life. I know of none who has actually done so, and thus I conclude that they're not serious in their beliefs.

As this may suggest, no, I don't find their arguments compelling -- quite the contrary. Again, like eliminative materialism and strict solipsism, the logic of their position is so constructed that it's unfalsifiable on its own terms, but that's no argument for the truth of a belief; as Karl Popper pointed out cogently, a statement that cannot be falsified by any conceivable evidence or argument is intellectual masturbation.

Of course, it's also relevant that I'm an occultist who has some reason to accept the reality of reincarnation. The existence of any form of afterlife confutes their attempt to equate death with an absence of experience and so renders the entire argument of Benatarian asymmetry absurd. There is ample evidence to support the claim that an afterlife is at least as likely as the nonexistence of the self after death, and it seems to me that the entire antinatalist position is rendered untenable by that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh, and I neglected to address your question about historical parallels. Yes, it's a very old belief that has been held by a variety of thinkers; you'll find ancient equivalents on the Indian subcontinent and among the more extreme Gnostics, and of course there's Schopenhauer, who made that same view the least convincing and least effectively justified element of his philosophy. Of course he was also a great example of my first point; his supposed conviction that all pleasure was merely an invitation to further pain and that life was unrelieved misery didn't keep him from indulging his desires for classical music, gourmet dining, and prostitutes, or from fleeing from Berlin to escape the epidemic that killed Hegel so that his supposedly melancholy existence would go on for years of further misery and despair!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I actually wrote a blog post (https://spellofwordshome.wordpress.com/2019/12/28/never-to-have-been-a-critique-of-benatars-antinatalism/) dissecting Benatar's brand of antinatalism recently. To summarize it: Benatar presents an incoherent idea of what it means for nonexistence to be better than existence, his asymmetry is supposedly based on a set of moral intuitions and yet is deeply unintuitive, and he contradicts his own philosophy by saying death is bad. This last point in particular applies to nearly every form of antinatalism I've encountered.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think Benatar is arguing from a false premise here--indeed, from a howlingly bad false premise.

He posits that pain is bad. Pain is in deed unpleasant, but it is very far from a bad thing. Pain (and any other sort of suffering) is one of the very best "thrust blocks" in existence that anyone has for leaping ahead into greater wisdom and kindness. There are good reasons why homespun philosophers think so highly of the "school of hard knocks."

In fact, I would rather stand his argument on its head and claim that a life without any pain or suffering would be one of the very worst sorts of life at all.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I believe Ligotti quotes Benatar in The Conspiracy Against the Human Race - if not Benatar, then another antinatalist philosopher.

In essence, Ligotti says that by having children, you become responsible for all the suffering they'll experience, and the suffering their children will experience, and so on.

But later in the book, he talks about determinism, in his quest to prove that human consciousness doesn't exist, and says that a determinist court of justice would not hold a murderer to be guilty of anything, because the murderer never had an actual choice.

Well, hold on:

1) If I have kids, I could argue that I'm not responsible for their suffering, my parents are. And really they're not, but their grandparents are - and so on and so on, into absurdity.

2) These two arguments are contradictory. If determinism obtains, then I am no more guilty of inflicting suffering on future generations than I am of committing murder, because whatever choice I may have is an illusion.

Ligotti's book is full of contradictory nonsense like this. Emotionally, it has the tone of an abuser trying to destroy your sense of reality while pleading concern for your well-being.

Also, Benatar's table there is nonsense on stilts. Reducing the complex spectra of human existence to a simplistic plus-minus scheme ought to be an omen of the long-delayed death of modern philosophy.

-Cliff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I remember noticing this danger when making to-do lists for myself years ago. I'd write four or five things that I really needed to get done, and but then I'd starting thinking, feeling more ambitious, and end up with ten or fifteen tasks, including some that were obviously not realistic. Eventually I'd catch myself adding some worthy task I'd had on a dozen lists before and knew perfectly well I wasn't going to do that day either, and realize that at that point the whole list was shot; now I might not even do the few things I'd set down to do in the first place! It was a to-do wish list. It was so strange to notice though, the kind of compulsion to list these things I meant to do or dreamt of doing even as I was admitting to myself that I couldn't or wouldn't do them. I started to recognize this process whereby I avoided mundane chores I could and ought to accomplish by grouping them with more ambitious, more interesting things that I knew I was in no danger of actually doing.
I talked with my sister about this process once (she'd had the same experience) and we decided it would be better to have two lists: a to-do list strictly for things that should and will get done asap, and a never-to-do list on which to safely put the things we "really should" or "would like" to do but know goddarn well we won't. The never-to-do list is great! It's wonderful to say, f it, I should do that but I'm not gonna. And the things I really do need to deal with have a place to sit and stare at me and make me uncomfortable. Meanwhile the to-do list is unsullied and I can get things crossed off it without the drama.
Just a memory that popped up when I read this thread. Have a good day, all!

Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Anorexia
replies.content:
(JMG: This isn't a question, so I hope you'll excuse the Tuesday post. If you don't put it through, I'll take the hint.)

I was a hardcore reductive materialist in high school. Then I dated a girl with Anorexia. When I became emotionally and energetically close to her, the perception of a malicious "thing" that was "made of consciousness" and at least semi-independent of her was clear and concrete enough that I was forced to philosophically reject materialism within hours of first becoming aware of it.

I was an active participant in the Angry Atheist scene (back when that was internet-cool). I knew all the aggressive rhetoric used to explain away spiritual things, and I steeped myself in the propaganda whenever I had the chance, but the experience was too visceral to deny.

I settled on the explanation that what I had experienced was a fractured part of her own consciousness, mostly because I found Chaos Magick literature and explanations the most palatable at first, but the line of consciousness existing independently of matter was crossed nonetheless.

It may be relevant to note that she was a participant in the Pro-Ana/Pro-Mia scene, which was thriving at the time.

Pro-Ana/Pro-Mia started out with collections of tips to avoid eating and hide purging, but lead into things like thinking of Ana and Mia as deities who would be disappointed in you for eating (or eating without purging), reciting prayers to them for help ignoring hunger pangs, and making blood offerings (from the self harm that was almost universal among high schoolers back then, at least in my experience) to them.

That sounds more dangerous to me now as a Ceremonial Magician than it did to me when I first learned about it as a Chaote, although I never did think it was a good idea.

Yesterday I decided to make a small ceremony of communion with the Sun, now that it's in Aries and partly to celebrate my own birthday which is today.

I don't have a kitchen in the room I rent, so I always eat from other places, and I found a new burger place near my house, with some excellent burgers. I did a geomantic chart to ask for the results of the ceremony (though retrospectively I should have done it before it and not when I got doubtful)and it had Carcer on the right, Laetitia on the left, and Tristitia as the judge. replies.content:

In this case, I'd see Carcer as representing the OTO group you are part of, Laetitia as the ritual, and Tristiatia as the result. In other words, the OTO group-- including you, insoffar as you are part of it-- is sharply constrained in their actions by their own limitations, which includes the culture of bullying and a--holery that is part of the OTO and the Internet generally. Encountering Joy in anyone, they do their best to turn it into Sorrow.

But, like I said, the whole thing is a misunderstanding. When you re-read the chart, do so with that in mind, and also with the following in mind:

2. The ritual worked perfectly. You called on the God. He responded, and you shared something together. You made the mistake of sharing it with a bunch of jerks-- but don't worry about it; they can't take it away from you. In your case I'd get out of the Facebook group that you probably used to share your experience and focus more on your magic and its results than what other people think, especially if those people are either 1. in the OTO, 2. on the Internet or, 3. and worst of all, both.

comments.title: Astrological Interpretation
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I've been trying to understand my natal chart, and how to relate it to my personality. I'm aware of your advice that the chart should be taken as a whole, and not to get hung up on any one part of it, so while I am trying to understand it piece-by piece, I am not stressing.

The situation is that I am a solar Libra, and Libras are supposed to be very social, and to put it bluntly, I am not. While mulling that over, the thought came to me that retrograde could mean recursive, that is operating on the self rather than being externally directed. With that in mind, I also have a retrograde Saturn and Mercury. Could these be interpreted as showing a tendency to self-limitation and self-analysis (in the manner of their signs and in the area of their houses)?

Am I on the right track with this.

Thanks!

Steve.

replies.title: Re: Astrological Interpretation
replies.content:
This is a great example of why sun-sign astrology is drivel. The position of your sun does not determine whether you're social or not. You need to look at Mercury, the planet of communication; Venus, the planet of pleasure and creativity; and Saturn, the planet of solitude and withdrawal, just for starters. You also need to examine the rulers of the third and fifth houses of your natal chart, and any planets that are in them.

That your Saturn and Mercury are both retrograde is a part of the puzzle, because those do tend to indicate a mind turned inward rather than outward. Here again, though, you have to combine that with all the other indications in your chart!

replies.title: wiccan magic
replies.content:
I'm going to jump in here, as a long time Witch. In studying three different systems (New Reformed Orthodox Order of the Golden Dawn, Kingstone and Gardnarian) and reading books about others such as Dianic and Fairy, I have yet to discover any distinctive Wiccan magic other than the concept of raising and releasing the cone of power. Since this is a group exercise involving dancing, chanting, etc. while concentrating on a goal and using a group cue to release the energy toward that goal, it isn't really practical to try alone. The bits and pieces of spell work in various books combine candle spells, poppet work, chanting, etc.--pretty much all from the folk magic spectrum. What JMG has described as the theories of E. Levi about magic being a matter of concentrating the will seems to be the basis for most of these. Much mention of focus, visualization and so forth. Witches also use a variety of divination: some do astrology, some tarot, some runes, etc.

If you are attracted to the god and goddess of the Craft or to the seasonal and monthly celebrations, the idea of working magic in a group of close friends, etc. then Wicca may be the path for you, but I can't honestly claim that there are any secret ways of working magic that you can't find elsewhere.

Now it seems to me that the type of magic JMG has described in his book on Natural Magic is an obvious path for Wiccans to adopt.

Rita

replies.title: Re: wiccan reading
replies.content:
It might sound strange, but "The Idiots Guide to Wicca and Witchcraft" is actually fairly sound and comprehensive. Although I have no idea where they got the weird stuff about dragons. The authors are Zimmermann and Gleason. "Her Hidden Children" by Chas Clifton is good on history of Craft in the US. Ignore Kelly's "Crafting the Art of Magick"; his speculations about Gerald Gardner do not stand up to basic logic. For more reading suggestions, "The Well-Read Witch", by McColman, though it was printed in 2002 and may be a bit out of date.

Rita

replies.title: (no subject)

My take is that Edward A. "Aleister" Crowley was a minor figure on the fringes of the English magical scene who would be completely forgotten today if Grady McMurtry, a much more serious magician, hadn't taken up Crowley's rambling legacy and turned it into a workable spiritual path. I have yet to see any evidence that the man could magic his way out of a wet paper bag; his supposed magical discoveries were all plagiarisms or pastiches of other people's work, and -- well, let's just say that if you start life rich, handsome, well educated, charismatic, and talented, and the result of a life spent practicing magic is that you end up as a burnt-out drug addict in a small town nursing home with an estate valued at 16 shillings and a name you've personally made a laughingstock on three continents, you're doing it wrong.

He did invent his version of the definition of magic you've mentioned; Fortune had to add the words "in consciousness" to make it useful, though.

replies.title: Re: Ritual practice
replies.content:

2) A little. I know there are Pagan groups in Poland and Lithuania, at least, who are reviving the worship of their ancient gods. There's also a Neopagan who goes by Ceiswr Serith who's done significant work into the Proto-Indo-European deities, insofar as those can be reconstructed.

replies.title: Re: Ritual practice
replies.content:
To judge both by the few surviving old sources and by recent folklore, all of Scandinavia and Eastern Europe was originally a patchwork quilt of entirely different pantheons and practices. Most likely this area was originally the home of a number of unrelated small tribes, each more or less equal in strength and size to any of the others; and they borrowed rather freely from one another.

(About a century ago, Franz Boas and his students showed pretty conclusively that a similar situation obtained among the Native Akmerican tribes in the Pacific Northwest. There are several distince families of languages there, as well as distinct genetic heritages and distinct cultures. But the boundaries between distinct langauge families, distinct cultural patterns, and distinct genetic ancestry do not align with one another.)

In Scandinavia and Eastern Europe there are now three distinct Indo-European language families: Germanic (Scandinavian), Baltic, and Slavic. But most of that region was previously settled by peoples who spoke a quite wide range of non-Indo-European languages, and--to judge by archeological evidence and by their descendants' folklore--had a quite wide range of cultures (including religious practices). In general, the pattern of Indo-European settlement there seems to have been one of coexistence and mutual adaptation rather than conquest and extermination.

All of which is a long-winded way of saying that there is no single ancestral pantheon to be reconstructed, but very many pantheons. You can choose and pick between them, of course.
